Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Ernst Mach

1838 – 1916

Neutral monism, the analysis of sensations, and the self as a “bundle of elements.”

Ernst Mach did not produce a “philosophy of mind” in the later analytic sense, but one can extract a remarkably coherent philosophy of mind from his work on sensations, physiological psychology, perception, scientific cognition, and the ego. The central thesis is this: mind and matter are not two substances, nor is one reducible to the other in the mechanical-materialist sense. They are two directions of inquiry into the same field of neutral “elements” — colors, sounds, pressures, spaces, times, pleasures, pains, memories, volitions, and their functional relations. The “self” is not an incorporeal owner of experience; it is a relatively stable, practically useful, biologically economical complex within that field. Bodies are also relatively stable complexes. The mind–body problem, for Mach, is largely generated by reifying both sides.

Mach’s philosophy of mind is therefore best classified as anti-substantialist, empiricist, naturalistic, biological, and neutral-monist, though each label needs qualification. It is anti-substantialist because it rejects mind, matter, ego, soul, and thing-in-itself as ultimate explanatory substances. It is empiricist because it begins from the elements given in experience. It is naturalistic because it treats psychical life, including science, as part of biological life. It is neutral-monist because the basic elements are not intrinsically mental or physical; they become “mental” or “physical” according to the functional relations under investigation.

A crucial interpretive warning is needed at the outset. Mach’s word “sensation” easily misleads modern readers. He does not mean private mental atoms locked inside a subject’s mind. He explicitly prefers the term “elements” because “sensation” already suggests a one-sided theory. The same color, for instance, is physical when considered in dependence on light, temperature, other colors, and spatial circumstances; it is psychological when considered in dependence on the retina and body. The difference is, in Mach’s compressed formula, “not the subject matter, but the direction” of investigation.

1. Intellectual setting: Mach between physics, psychophysics, biology, and philosophy

Mach’s thought emerges from the nineteenth-century transformation of psychology into an experimental and physiological science. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Mach studied the relation between sensations and external stimuli, and that space, time, color, and sound — once treated primarily as physical categories — were being reconceived as both physical-world features and elements of inner experience. Mach was deeply influenced by Gustav Fechner’s psychophysics, and modern psychologists have regarded him as a founder or forerunner of Gestalt theory and as a discoverer of neural inhibition.

This background matters because Mach’s philosophy of mind is not an armchair theory imposed on psychology from outside. It is generated by concrete problems in perception: visual contrast, spatial perception, binocular depth, vestibular orientation, bodily motion, and the physiological conditions of sensory fields. The same source emphasizes that, although Mach became famous among philosophers for physics and philosophy of science, psychology was the primary driving force behind his philosophy of science.

Mach’s anti-materialism must also be read carefully. He is not anti-naturalistic and not a defender of supernatural mind. Rather, he is anti-mechanistic: he rejects the nineteenth-century tendency to think that nature must ultimately be understood as matter in motion, with psychical phenomena somehow squeezed out of moving particles. His naturalism is evolutionary and biological: there is nothing beyond nature, but nature is not adequately represented by the mechanical image of billiard-ball matter.

This is why Mach’s psychological project is inseparable from his critique of metaphysics. At the beginning of The Analysis of Sensations, he observes that physical science had become dominant enough to impose its methods on the physiology of the senses, but he warns that physics is only part of a larger body of knowledge and cannot exhaust the subject matter. The physiology of the senses must use physics, but it must not be swallowed by a narrowly physicalistic conceptual scheme.

2. The basic ontology: elements, complexes, and functional relations

Mach begins from what he calls elements: colors, sounds, temperatures, pressures, spaces, times, memories, feelings, volitions, pleasures, pains, and motor sensations. These are not “mental contents” in the Cartesian sense; they are the primitive material of both physical and psychological description. They are not hidden behind experience; they are what must be organized, related, and functionally described.

From these elements, relatively stable complexes emerge. A table, a tree, a friend, a body, and an ego are all complexes whose relative permanence allows them to receive names. Ordinary language and practical life privilege these stable complexes because doing so is economical: we cannot constantly track every variation in color, pressure, temperature, angle, bodily condition, and memory. A “thing” is thus not an unknowable substance behind sensory qualities; it is a practically stabilized complex of elements.

This gives Mach a distinctive answer to the old metaphysical question: do bodies produce sensations? His answer is no, at least not if “body” means a hidden material nucleus that causes inner mental states. Bodies do not stand behind sensations as occult producers of them. Rather, bodies are themselves complexes of elements. The supposed hidden “nucleus” is an economical mental symbol we introduce after becoming accustomed to recurring patterns.

Mach’s analysis of “appearance” and “reality” follows from the same point. The bent-looking stick in water and the straight-feeling stick in touch are not one false appearance and one metaphysical reality. They are differently conditioned facts. Dreaming and waking are likewise not distinguished by a metaphysical gulf; waking experience is more stable, connected, and practically important. “Reality” is not what hides behind experience, but the more stable, coherent, functionally integrated order of elements.

This is why Mach is not a simple subjective idealist. He does not say: “only my private ideas exist.” He says that the old opposition between inner sensation and outer thing is a bad abstraction. When we examine the functional dependencies among elements, we no longer need to posit unknowable things-in-themselves on one side and an equally mysterious ego on the other.

3. The mind–body relation: not dualism, not reductive materialism, but neutral functionalism over elements

Mach’s most important contribution to philosophy of mind is his way of dissolving the mind–body problem. Traditional dualism posits two fundamentally different kinds of reality: mind and matter. Mechanistic materialism tries to reduce psychical phenomena to matter in motion. Subjective idealism risks making the material world dependent on mind. Mach rejects all three strategies as products of reified abstractions.

For Mach, the same element may belong to a physical complex in one relational context and a psychological complex in another. A color considered in relation to its luminous source and surrounding colors is a physical object. The same color considered in relation to the retina, optic nerve, body, and sensory field is a psychological object. This is not a change in the intrinsic nature of the color; it is a change in the direction of inquiry.

This position is close to what later came to be called neutral monism. Neutral monism holds that ultimate reality is of one kind, neither intrinsically mental nor intrinsically material, while mind and matter are derivative from more basic neutral entities. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy identifies Mach, William James, and Bertrand Russell as the three major figures in the traditional genealogy of neutral monism, and says that Mach occupies a central position in its history.

Mach’s version is especially functional and scientific. The “mental” is not a ghostly inner stuff; the “physical” is not hidden matter-in-itself. Both are lawful groupings of elements. Physics studies relatively stable relations among elements while abstracting from the special role of the observer’s body. Physiological psychology studies those same elements in relation to the body, sense organs, nervous system, movement, and perception.

This is why Mach can say that physiological psychology is, in the widest sense, continuous with physics, while also rejecting any crude attempt to explain feeling by the motion of brain atoms. The problem “how can atom motions produce feeling?” is, for Mach, badly framed. It assumes from the start that physics has already described a complete material world and that feeling must then be added as a mysterious product. Mach reverses the order: physics itself is an economical abstraction from the wider field of elements, and psychology investigates the same field under different dependencies.

This is not the later mind–brain identity theory. The identity theory, associated especially with U. T. Place and Herbert Feigl in the 1950s, identifies mental states and processes with brain states and processes. Mach does not say that sensations are brain processes. He says that both “brain” and “sensation” are complexes or functional organizations of elements, and that the problem is to understand their lawful interconnections without reifying either side.

Nor is Mach a straightforward functionalist in the late twentieth-century sense. Contemporary functionalism defines mental states by their roles in a cognitive system, especially by relations among sensory inputs, other mental states, and behavior. Mach anticipates functionalism insofar as he classifies mental and physical phenomena by relational dependence, but he is not a computational or causal-role functionalist. His basic units are qualitative and experiential elements, not abstract machine states.

4. Perception: active, relational, embodied, and anti-representationalist

Mach’s philosophy of mind is built from perception upward. He rejects the view that sense organs simply transmit a ready-made external world to a passive mind. His studies of Mach bands showed that perceived light and darkness at boundaries are not passive copies of external luminance. The senses preprocess information; contrast, boundary enhancement, and relational organization are already operative before higher judgment.

This is why Mach becomes a forerunner of Gestalt psychology. In studying Mach bands, he concluded that sensation is structured and relational; the mind and senses actively contribute to what is perceived. Christian von Ehrenfels, a founder of Gestalt theory, explicitly reviewed Mach’s Gestalt-related ideas in The Analysis of Sensations.

Mach’s position is therefore anti-direct-representationalist. There is no simple isomorphism between world and appearance. This does not mean that perception is worthless or merely subjective. It means that perception is a biologically organized field of relations, contrasts, and functional dependencies. What appears is real as an element; error enters when we misclassify how that element is connected with other elements.

Spatial perception is one of Mach’s central examples. He distinguishes physiological space from geometrical space. Physiological space is constructed by cognitive and bodily organization; it is finite, non-uniform, affectively loaded, and tied to directions such as up, down, left, right, near, and far. Geometrical space is an intellectual construction abstracted from such experience. Neither is an absolute container existing independently of all experiential and practical conditions.

His lecture on why humans have two eyes gives a concrete example. Depth perception is made possible by the cooperation of two eyes, which receive different images. The body is not an incidental container for perception; it is structurally implicated in how the world is spatially disclosed. Mach’s playful remark about “little geometricians” in the eyes captures the serious point: geometry begins as embodied perceptual skill before it becomes explicit mathematical science.

Mach’s work on orientation and balance deepens this embodied account. He studied sensations of movement, acceleration, verticality, falling, and rotation, and he connected them with bodily pressures, the labyrinth, semicircular canals, and otolith organs. Orientation is not simply visual; it is multisensory, vestibular, tactile, muscular, and dynamically related to bodily motion.

These investigations give Mach’s philosophy of mind a strongly embodied character. The body is not merely an object among objects; it is the special complex whose changes have unusually extensive effects on the rest of the experiential field. But this privilege of the body is functional, not metaphysical. The body is a powerful node in the network of elements, not the prison of a soul or the producer of an inner mental theater.

5. The ego: a practical, economical, biologically useful construction

Mach’s treatment of the ego is one of the most radical parts of his psychology. The ego is a complex of memories, moods, feelings, habits, bodily associations, and continuities. It is relatively permanent, but not absolutely permanent. Its continuity depends on slow change, memory, bodily habit, and the stable recurrence of environmental relations.

The ego is therefore not the owner of experience. Mach’s formula is that the “primary fact is not the ego” but the elements. The “I” is constituted by the organization of elements; it is not an independent subject standing behind them. Asking who possesses the sensations is, for Mach, a relapse into an older habit of thought in which every experience must be assigned to an unanalysed owner.

This is close to a bundle theory of the self, but not exactly Humean in the standard sense. Mach’s ego is a bundle or complex, but the elements composing it are not merely private mental impressions. They are the same neutral elements that also compose bodies and the world. The ego is a more strongly coherent region of the field, not a separate mental substance.

The ego’s boundaries are indefinite and movable. In ordinary life, we distinguish “my body,” “my pain,” “my memory,” and “external object” for practical purposes. But these borders shift. A tool may become functionally incorporated into action. A pain may dominate the field and narrow the ego. Scientific thought may expand the field beyond personal life. Mach even allows the ego to be extended, in principle, until it embraces the world, though he does not mean this as mystical self-expansion; he means that the border between ego and world is not metaphysically fixed.

The ego is an “ideal mental-economical unity,” formed because the organism needs to avoid pain, seek pleasure, protect the body, identify stable threats and affordances, and coordinate action. The self is thus biologically and pragmatically necessary, but philosophically dangerous when mistaken for an ultimate entity.

This position has ethical consequences. Mach thinks that giving up the ego as an ultimate unity can weaken exaggerated egoism, narrow nationalism, and the overvaluation of personal immortality. He does not deny the practical importance of personal identity; he denies its metaphysical ultimacy. The content of consciousness — scientific, artistic, social, and cultural content — matters more than the personal thread on which it is strung.

6. Soul, incorporeal mind, and immortality

Mach’s remarks on the soul are not extensive in the manner of theological treatises, but they are decisive enough. He rejects the old attempt to preserve psychical unity by finding a point in the brain as the “seat of the soul.” He treats that search as a crude reification produced by the habit of treating the ego as an indivisible unity.

Mach also rejects the spatial localization of sensations inside the head. The perception of a large tree does not need to “fit” inside the small brain, because the sensory field and the perceived head belong to the same field of elements. Sensations are not little objects stored spatially inside a cranial container. To think so is to confuse physiological correlation with metaphysical containment.

Secondary scholarship is explicit that Mach was an atheist and disbelieved in a soul, while still accepting a biologically oriented notion of direction or development in nature. This is important: his denial of the soul is not a reduction to dead mechanism. It is part of a broader attempt to understand psychical life as natural, biological, relational, and continuous with the rest of experience.

On death, Mach’s view is equally anti-substantialist. Since the ego is not a real unity, death is not the annihilation of a metaphysical soul-substance. It is the dissolution of a familiar association of elements. The personal ego ceases as a practical unity, but much of its content — except for many merely private memories — may persist in others through science, art, language, culture, and social life.

Thus Mach rejects individual immortality in the traditional sense. He does not replace it with a supernatural collective mind. He offers instead an impersonal continuity of contents: what is valuable in a person can survive through its incorporation into wider human life. The “person” is not metaphysically ultimate; the evolving content of consciousness and culture is more significant.

This places Mach against both Cartesian substance dualism and spiritualist conceptions of incorporeal mind. But he is also against reductive materialism, because he does not think a soul can be eliminated by simply identifying mind with brain matter. For him, both “soul” and “matter” are provisional concepts formed for limited purposes; philosophy must return to the relations among elements from which both abstractions arise.

7. Other minds: analogy without metaphysical abyss

Mach’s treatment of other minds is subtle. He recognizes that we do not directly perceive another person’s sensations in the same way we perceive their body. We infer or “add” the psychical side by analogy, connecting other bodies, expressions, movements, and actions with sensations and feelings similar to our own.

But he denies that this creates a deep metaphysical abyss. We also complete physical facts by analogy when we infer hidden nervous processes or unobserved physical relations. The inference to other minds is less familiar and less secure than some physical inferences, but it is not different in principle.

This is a powerful middle position. Mach is not a solipsist, because he treats other bodies, expressions, and psychical analogues as part of the same field of experience and scientific completion. But he is not a naive realist about other minds either; he acknowledges that the psychical life of others is functionally supplied by analogy.

He also avoids behaviorism. He does not reduce other minds to observable behavior. Rather, he says that behavior, expression, and movement are the physical side from which we infer feelings, thoughts, and wills. The aim of science is to disclose the sequence of facts between expression, movement, feeling, and thought.

8. Will, action, emotion, and bodily expression

Mach treats will and action within the same framework of elements and functional relations. A willed movement is not an occult act of a mental substance pushing the body. It is first a represented movement in one domain, connected under appropriate conditions with executed movement in another. The distinction between imagining, intending, and doing is a distinction among complexes and connections of elements, not a gulf between soul and body.

Pleasure and pain are also not separate from sensation. Mach argues that pleasure and pain can be treated as sensations, though they are less well analyzed and less sharply localized than ordinary sensory qualities. They are essential components of emotion. Emotion is therefore not an immaterial state floating above the body; it is a distributed organization of affective, sensory, bodily, and action-oriented elements.

Mach discusses William James and Théodule Ribot approvingly insofar as they connect emotion with bodily tendencies to action, though Mach refuses Ribot’s suggestion that the psychical is merely added onto the physical. For Mach, that distinction is precisely what must be overcome: the psychical and physical are not two independent layers, but two modes of organizing the same field.

Ideas themselves are practical and volitional. In Popular Scientific Lectures, Mach says that ideas illuminate the paths of the will and form part of life’s adaptation to an expanding sphere of action. Thought is not a detached mirror but a biological instrument for orientation, anticipation, selection, and coordinated action.

9. Cognition, science, and mental economy

Mach’s theory of mind cannot be separated from his theory of knowledge. Cognition is a biological adaptation. Science continues the organism’s effort to orient itself in a changing environment, but does so consciously, methodically, socially, and economically.

The economy of thought is central. Because the human mind is limited and the world is inexhaustibly rich, thought must simplify, abbreviate, symbolize, classify, and anticipate. Language, concepts, laws, mathematical symbols, and scientific theories all save experience. They allow one person’s experience to replace another’s direct trial, and they allow future facts to be anticipated rather than encountered blindly.

Scientific laws, on this view, are not metaphysical necessities written into nature. They are economical descriptions of stable relations among elements. Cause and effect are salient features selected for the mental reproduction of events. Substance is likewise a stabilizing habit of thought: we form it because we need permanence amid change.

This has direct implications for philosophy of mind. The concepts “mind,” “body,” “matter,” “soul,” “ego,” “cause,” and “substance” are not self-validating. They are tools. When they orient us successfully, they are useful; when they generate insoluble pseudo-problems, they must be revised or discarded.

Mach’s biologized epistemology also changes the status of the a priori. What looks necessary may be the sediment of biological and cultural adaptation. The organism inherits or acquires stable ways of organizing experience, and science then refines them. This is why Mach can reinterpret Kantian themes naturalistically: not as timeless structures imposed by a transcendental subject, but as historically and biologically formed habits of orientation.

Mach’s account of prejudice is especially revealing. He treats prejudice as a kind of intellectual reflex: often indispensable for life and science because no organism can re-judge everything from scratch, but dangerous when habitual judgment overrides new facts. Mental life is thus an adaptive balance between stable habit and revisable transformation.

10. The resulting philosophy of mind, stated systematically

A comprehensive Machian philosophy of mind can be extracted as the following set of theses.

First, the fundamental data are elements, not minds, bodies, brains, souls, or things-in-themselves. Colors, sounds, spaces, pressures, pains, pleasures, memories, motor sensations, and volitions are the starting points of inquiry. They are not yet classified as mental or physical in themselves.

Second, mental and physical classifications are relational. An element is psychological when investigated in dependence on the body, sense organs, nervous system, memory, and action. The same element is physical when investigated in dependence on other elements in stable external relations.

Third, the self is a practical unity, not a substance. It is a relatively stable complex of memories, feelings, moods, habits, and bodily relations. Its continuity matters for life, action, ethics, and social organization, but it is not metaphysically ultimate.

Fourth, the body is privileged functionally, not ontologically. The body is the complex whose alterations most deeply and widely affect the rest of the field. Closing the eyes, pressing the eye, severing a nerve, changing posture, rotating, falling, or accelerating changes the field of elements in distinctive ways. But this does not make the body a hidden material cause of inner sensations; it makes it a central node of functional dependence.

Fifth, perception is active and relational. Senses do not passively copy a ready-made world. They contrast, select, preprocess, organize, and coordinate. Spatiality, depth, verticality, and objecthood are structured by bodily and sensory systems.

Sixth, consciousness is not an independent entity. Mach does not need a special metaphysical “consciousness” substance or container. What exists are elements and their connections, some of which form the complexes we call presentations, memories, emotions, volitions, and selves.

Seventh, other minds are known by analogical completion from bodily expression and action. This is not an occult leap into a separate realm but a scientific-practical inference continuous with other cases in which we complete partial experience.

Eighth, thought is adaptive anticipation. Concepts, hypotheses, scientific laws, and language are economical instruments for reproducing, organizing, and anticipating facts. Mental life is continuous with biological life.

Ninth, the soul is a reification. There is no incorporeal owner of experience, no indivisible ego-substance, and no scientifically useful “seat of the soul.” What the soul was meant to explain — unity, continuity, agency, feeling, memory, personal identity — must be explained by the organization of elements and their bodily, social, and biological relations.

Tenth, the mind–body problem is to be dissolved rather than solved in dualist or reductive terms. If “mind” and “body” are treated as ultimate substances, the problem appears insoluble. If they are treated as economical complexes in one field of elements, the apparent gulf disappears, leaving empirical questions about functional interdependence.

11. Mach’s historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Historically, Mach belongs to a decisive transitional moment. Before him, philosophy of mind was often dominated by the inherited categories of soul, subject, consciousness, representation, matter, and appearance. In the nineteenth century, physiology, psychophysics, evolutionary biology, and experimental psychology began to destabilize those categories. Mach stands exactly at that intersection.

Against Cartesian dualism, Mach denies that there are two substances, mind and body. Against spiritualism, he rejects the soul as an explanatory entity. Against crude materialism, he denies that psychical life can be explained by beginning with atoms or matter in motion and then asking how feeling is produced. Against subjective idealism and solipsism, he insists that the world is not merely a private mental construction; rather, the ego/world contrast is itself a practical abstraction from a larger field of elements.

In relation to Kant, Mach accepts the need to examine the conditions under which experience is organized, but he naturalizes those conditions. Space, causality, objecthood, and selfhood are not treated as fixed transcendental structures of a pure subject. They are biologically, physiologically, historically, and economically formed modes of orientation. Mach’s own autobiographical remark about encountering Kant’s Prolegomena and later finding the thing-in-itself superfluous is central to this development.

In relation to Hume, Mach shares the anti-substantialist tendency: the self is not discovered as a simple entity behind experience. But Mach’s framework is more physiological and scientific than Hume’s standard bundle theory. The elements are not merely impressions in a private stream; they are the material of physics and psychology alike.

In relation to Fechner and psychophysics, Mach radicalizes the thought that the physical and psychical can be related mathematically and neutrally. Fechner’s work showed that sensations could be measured and related to external stimulation; Mach develops this into a broader epistemology in which physical and psychical concepts must be unified through elements and functional dependencies.

In relation to Gestalt psychology, Mach is an important precursor. His work on Mach bands, relational perception, spatial form, and melody-like structures helped undermine atomistic and passive-copy models of perception. The senses organize; wholes and relations matter.

In relation to neutral monism, Mach is foundational. The later tradition running through William James and Bertrand Russell inherits the idea that mind and matter are not ultimate opposed substances but different functional groupings of a more basic neutral field. James develops this as “pure experience,” and Russell later develops neutral monism in the language of logical construction and events.

Mach also influenced logical empiricism and the Vienna Circle, though the relation is complex. Moritz Schlick was appointed to Mach’s old chair at Vienna, the Verein Ernst Mach was created to disseminate scientific ways of thought, and Mach’s anti-metaphysical precept was accepted before the Circle’s more formal verification principle took shape. Later scholarship warns against reducing Mach to the simplified image of logical positivism, but the historical link is undeniable.

In the longer development of twentieth-century philosophy of mind, Mach is neither a behaviorist, nor an identity theorist, nor a standard functionalist. Behaviorism tends to avoid or reduce inner experience to behavior; Mach preserves experience but reconceives it non-dualistically. Identity theory identifies mental processes with brain processes; Mach treats both as complexes within a wider field. Functionalism defines mental states by causal role; Mach anticipates role-dependence but retains qualitative elements as basic.

Mach’s importance today lies partly in the renewed interest in neutral monism, Russellian monism, panqualityism, and non-reductive accounts of the mind–body relation. Contemporary neutral monism is not merely historical; it remains part of ongoing discussion about how mental and physical phenomena can arise from, or be grounded in, a more basic reality.

12. Strengths of Mach’s philosophy of mind

Mach’s first strength is that he avoids the false choice between dualism and reductive materialism. He does not posit a soul, but he also refuses to treat experience as an inexplicable by-product of matter. He instead questions the conceptual architecture that makes the problem look insoluble.

His second strength is his integration of philosophy with scientific psychology. His analysis is not merely speculative. It grows from visual contrast, spatial perception, binocular vision, balance, orientation, bodily movement, and the physiology of sense organs. This gives his philosophy of mind an empirical seriousness that many purely metaphysical theories lack.

His third strength is his relational theory of perception. By denying that perception is a passive copy, he anticipates central themes in Gestalt psychology, ecological psychology, embodied cognition, and contemporary perceptual science. The perceived world is structured by organismic capacities without becoming merely subjective fantasy.

His fourth strength is his critique of the ego. Modern philosophy, psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science have repeatedly challenged the idea of a simple, indivisible self. Mach’s account of the ego as a stable but revisable construction of memory, affect, body, and habit anticipates many later anti-substantialist accounts of personal identity.

His fifth strength is his account of cognition as economical adaptation. It connects concept formation, scientific theory, language, memory, and practical action under a unified biological model. This gives a powerful explanation of why humans form simplified concepts, why those concepts work, and why they later become obstacles.

13. Difficulties and unresolved problems

The main difficulty is the status of the elements themselves. Are they experiences? Qualities? Events? Neutral entities? If they are too closely tied to experience, Mach risks collapsing into phenomenalism. If they are made too neutral, it becomes harder to explain why they appear as experience at all. This difficulty remains one of the enduring challenges for neutral monism generally.

A second difficulty concerns the unity of consciousness. Mach argues that the subject is built from sensations and that ego-consciousness comes in degrees. That is powerful, but it leaves open how momentary elements are unified into a single field of awareness at a time, and how that field differs from merely external aggregation.

A third difficulty concerns intentionality. Mach can explain ideas as economical anticipations and instruments of adaptation, but he gives less detailed analysis of how a thought is about an absent object, a possible state of affairs, a mathematical structure, or a false proposition. James and Russell develop this issue more explicitly in later neutral-monist frameworks.

A fourth difficulty concerns realism about unobservables. Mach’s suspicion of atoms as metaphysical reifications was methodologically intelligible in his context, but later scientific developments made atomism enormously fruitful. This does not wholly refute his philosophy of mind, but it pressures his general tendency to treat unobservables as economical tools rather than robust theoretical posits.

A fifth difficulty concerns normativity. If cognition is adaptation and economy, what distinguishes truth from useful simplification? Mach can answer that successful orientation, stability, and functional integration matter; but critics may say that truth cannot be reduced to biological economy. His account is therefore strongest as a naturalized epistemology, less complete as a theory of truth.

14. Final synthesis

Mach’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one sentence: mental life is not the activity of an incorporeal soul, nor the secretion of a material brain, but the organism-centered organization of neutral experiential elements into relatively stable, biologically useful, economically describable complexes.

Its ontology is neutral elements. Its psychology is physiological and relational. Its theory of perception is active and embodied. Its theory of self is anti-substantialist and pragmatic. Its account of cognition is biological economy. Its treatment of the soul is eliminative, but not crudely materialist. Its solution to the mind–body problem is dissolution through conceptual reconstruction: once “mind” and “matter” are seen as economical organizations of the same field, the supposed metaphysical gulf loses its force.

Mach’s historical significance is therefore larger than his reputation as a positivist philosopher of science. He stands at the junction of nineteenth-century psychophysics, evolutionary naturalism, anti-metaphysical empiricism, Gestalt psychology, neutral monism, and later analytic philosophy. For the philosophy of mind, his lasting contribution is the attempt to think mind without soul, matter without metaphysical material substance, perception without passive copying, and selfhood without an indivisible ego.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
